elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper we prove that the Gromov--Hausdorff distance between $\R^n$ and its subset $A$ is finite if and only if $A$ is an $\e$-net in $\R^n$ for some $\e>0$.
elements.sent-2411-002: For infinite-dimensional Euclidean spaces this is not true.
elements.sent-2411-003: The proof is essentially based on upper estimate of the Euclidean Gromov--Hausdorff distance by means of the Gromov-Hausdorff distance.
elements.sent-2411-005: Traditionally, the Gromov--Hausdorff distance is used for bounded metric spaces, mainly, for compact ones.
elements.sent-2411-006: In the case of non-bounded metric spaces, this distance is applied to define the pointed Gromov--Hausdorff convergence, and besides, there were a few attempts to define the corresponding distance function in this case, see for instance~\cite{Herron .}
elements.sent-2411-007: Since the Gromov--Hausdorff distance between isometric metric spaces vanishes, it is natural to identify such spaces in this theory.
elements.sent-2411-008: Thus, the main space for investigating the Gromov--Hausdorff distance is the space $\cM$ of non-empty compact metric spaces considered upto isometry, endowed with the Gromov--Hausdorff distance.
elements.sent-2411-009: Here the distance function is a metric, and this metric is complete, separable, geodesic, etc., see for details~\cite{BBI , \cite{GromovEng .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: In~\cite{GromovEng , M.\,Gromov described some geometric properties of the Gromov--Hausdorff distance on the space $\GH$ of all non-empty metric spaces, not necessarily bounded, considered upto isometry.}
elements.sent-2411-011: It is easy to see that $\GH$ is not a set, but a proper class in terms of von Neumann--Bernays--G\"odel set theory.
elements.sent-2411-012: M.\,Gromov suggested to consider subclasses consisting of all metric spaces on finite distance between each other.
elements.sent-2411-014: M.\,Gromov announced that the clouds are obviously complete and contractible~\cite{GromovEng .}
elements.sent-2411-015: He suggested to see that on the example of the cloud containing $\R^n$.
elements.sent-2411-016: The idea is to consider a mapping of $\GH$ into itself that for each $X\in\GH$, multiplies all distances in $X$ by some real $\l>0$.
elements.sent-2411-017: It is easy to see that such mapping takes $\R^n$ to an isometric metric space, i.e., $\R^n$ is a fixed point of this mapping.
elements.sent-2411-018: However, the Gromov--Hausdorff distances from $\R^n$ to all other metrics spaces in its cloud are multiplied by $\l$ as well.
elements.sent-2411-019: It remains to see what happens when $\l\to0+$.
elements.sent-2411-020: Nevertheless, these ``obvious observations'' lead to a few questions.
elements.sent-2411-021: To start with, the clouds are proper classes (B.\,Nesterov, private conversations); contractibility is a topological notion; so, to speak about contractibility of a cloud, we have to define a topology on it.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, it is not possible to introduce a topology on a proper class, because the proper class cannot be an element of any other class by definition, but each topological space is an element of its own topology.
elements.sent-2411-023: In~\cite{BogatyTuzhilin by S.\,A.\,Bogaty and A.\,A.\,Tuzhilin, the authors developed a convenient language that allows to avoid the set-theoretic problems.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Namely, they introduced an analog of topology on so-called set-filtered classes (each set belongs to this family, together with the class $\GH$) and defined continuous mappings between such classes.
elements.sent-2411-025: At the same time, the authors of~\cite{BogatyTuzhilin found examples of metric spaces that jump onto infinite Gromov--Hausdorff distance after multiplying their distance functions on some $\l>0$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Thus, the multiplication on such $\l$ does not map the clouds into themselves.
elements.sent-2411-027: This strange behavior of clouds increased the interest to them, see~\cite{BogatyTuzhilin for details.}
elements.sent-2411-028: In the present paper we continue investigation of the geometry of the standard (non-pointed) Gromov--Hausdorff distance between non-bounded metric spaces.
elements.sent-2411-029: It is well-known that each $\e$-net of a metric space $X$ is on finite Gromov--Hausdorff distance from $X$.
elements.sent-2411-030: Is the converse statement true as well? Example~\ref{examplel2 shows that it is not true even for infinite-dimensional Euclidean spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-031: In the present paper we prove that the converse statement holds for finite-dimensional Euclidean spaces.
elements.sent-2411-032: A natural question is to understand what happens for other finite-dimensional normed spaces.
elements.sent-2411-033: It turns out that there are a few obstacles to obtain such generalizations.
elements.sent-2411-034: Firstly, our approach is not completely straightforward.
elements.sent-2411-035: In particularly, it is based on Theorem~2 from paper~\cite{Memoli by F.\,Memoli.}
elements.sent-2411-036: This inequality provides an upper estimate of the Euclidean Gromov--Hausdorff distance\/ in terms of the classical Gromov--Hausdorff distance.
elements.sent-2411-037: The effectiveness of this theorem is based on the richness of the isometry group $\Iso(\R^n)$ of $\R^n$, that is not the case for other finite-dimensional normed spaces.
elements.sent-2411-038: Secondly, we might consider some $\e$-net $\s$ in $\R^n$ and try to approximate it in terms of the Gromov--Hausdorff distance with an $\e'$-net $\s'$ in a finite-dimensional normed space $X$.
elements.sent-2411-039: However, according to paper~\cite{MikhailovYoung , it holds $\dist_{GH (\s,\,\s')=\infty$ unless $X$ is isometric to $\R^n$, so it is also impossible to transfer our result to such $X$ using this idea.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Summing up, the question whether or not a subset $Y$ of a finite-dimensional normed space $X$ on a finite Gromov--Hausdorff distance from $X$ is an $\e$-net in $X$ for some $\e>0$ remains open.
elements.sent-2411-041: We thank professor A.\,O.\,Ivanov for permanent attention to the work.
elements.sent-2411-042: We also thank the team of the seminar ``Theory of extremal networks'' leaded by A.\,O.\,Ivanov and A.\,A.\,Tuzhilin.

elements.sent-2411-044: Let $B_r(a)=\{x\in X:|ax|\le r\}$ and $S_r(a)=\{x\in X:|ax|=r\}$ be the closed ball and the sphere of radius $r$ centered at the point $a$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-045: For an arbitrary subset $A\ss X$, its closure in $X$ is denoted by $\bA$.
elements.sent-2411-046: For non-empty subsets $A\ss X$ and $B\ss X$, we put $\dist(A,\,B)=\inf\bigl\{|ab|:\,a\in A,\,b\in B\bigl\}$.
elements.sent-2411-047: Let $A$ and $B$ be non-empty subsets of a metric space $X$.
elements.sent-2411-048: The Hausdorff distance between $A$ and $B$ is the value \begin{equation}\dist_H(A,\,B)=\inf\bigl\{r>0:A\ss B_r(B),\,B\ss B_r(A)\bigr\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-050: If $X',\,Y'$ are subsets of a metric space $Z$ such that $X'$ is isometric to $X$ and $Y'$ is isometric to $Y$, then we call the triple $(X',\,Y',\,Z)$ a metric realization of the pair $(X,\,Y)$.
elements.sent-2411-051: The Gromov--Hausdorff distance $\dist_{GH(X,\,Y)$} between two metric spaces $X$, $Y$ is the infimum of positive numbers $r$ such that there exists a metric realization $(X',\,Y',\,Z)$ of the pair $(X,\,Y)$ with $\dist_H(X',\,Y') \le r$.
elements.sent-2411-053: Recall that any subset $\s\ss X\x Y$ is called a relation between $X$ and $Y$.
elements.sent-2411-054: Denote the set of all non-empty relations between $X$ and $Y$ by $\cP_0(X,\,Y)$.
elements.sent-2411-055: We put \begin{equation}\pi_X\:X\x Y\to X,\;\pi_X(x,\,y)=x,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\pi_Y\:X\x Y\to Y,\;\pi_Y(x,\,y)=y.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-056: A relation $R\ss X\x Y$ is called a correspondence\/ if $\pi_X|_R$ and $\pi_Y|_R$ are surjective.
elements.sent-2411-057: In other words, correspondences are multivalued surjective mappings.
elements.sent-2411-058: Denote the set of all correspondences between $X$ and $Y$ by $\cR(X,\,Y)$.
elements.sent-2411-060: We use the following standard notation \begin{equation}R(A) = \{y\in Y\,|\,\exists\,x\in A\:(x,\,y)\in R\},\end{equation} \begin{equation}R^{-1}(B) = \{x\in X\,|\,\exists\,y\in B\:(x,\,y)\in R\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-062: Then for every $\s\in\cP_0(X,\,Y)$, the distortion of $\s$ is defined as \begin{equation}\dis\s=\sup\Bigl\{\bigl||xx'|-|yy'|\bigr|:(x,\,y),\,(x',\,y')\in\s\Bigr\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-063: Recall that for any sets $X$, $Y$, $Z$, and relations $\s_1\in\cP_0(X,\,Y)$, $\s_2\in\cP_0(Y,\,Z)$, the composition of $\s_1$ and $\s_2$, denoted by $\s_2\c\s_1$, is the set of all $(x,\,z)\in X\x Z$ for which there exists $y\in Y$ such that $(x,\,y)\in\s_1$ and $(y,\,z)\in\s_2$.
elements.sent-2411-064: Given non-empty subsets $A$ and $B$ of a metric space $X$ with $\dist_H(A,\,B)<c$, the set $U=\{(a,\,b)\in A\x B:|ab|<c\}$ is a correspondence between $A$ and $B$ such that $\dis U\le 2c$.
elements.sent-2411-065: The inequality $\dist_H(A,\,B)<c$ implies that for an arbitrary $a\in A$, there exists $b\in B$ such that $|ab|<c$.
elements.sent-2411-066: Hence, $(a,\,b)\in U$ and, therefore, the projection of $U$ to $A$ is surjective.
elements.sent-2411-067: Similarly, the projection of $U$ to $B$ is surjective.
elements.sent-2411-068: Thus, $U$ is a correspondence between $A$ and $B$.
elements.sent-2411-071: From now on, we suppose that $\R^n$ is always endowed with the standard Euclidean norm.
elements.sent-2411-072: Denote by $\Iso(\R^n)$ the group of all isometries of $\R^n$.
elements.sent-2411-073: For arbitrary non-empty subsets $X,\,Y\ss\R^n$, the Euclidean Gromov--Hausdorff distance\/ is \begin{equation}\dist_{EH}(X,\,Y)=\inf_{T\in \Iso(\R^n)}\dist_{H}\bigl(X,\,T(Y)\bigr).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-074: Then \begin{equation}\dist_{GH}(X,\,Y)\le \dist_{EH}(X,\,Y)\le c_n'\cdot M^{\frac12}\cdot\bigl(\dist_{GH}(X,\,Y)\bigr)^{\frac12},\end{equation} where $M=\max\bigl\{\diam(X),\,\diam(Y)\bigr\}$ and $c_n'$ is a constant that depends only on $n$.
elements.sent-2411-076: Then \begin{equation}\dist_{GH}(X,\,Y)\le\dist_{EH}(X,\,Y)\le c_n'\cdot M^{\frac12}\cdot\bigl(\dist_{GH}(X,\,Y)\bigr)^{\frac12},\end{equation} where $M=\max\bigl\{\diam(X),\,\diam(Y)\bigr\}$ and $c_n'$ is a constant that depends only on $n$.
elements.sent-2411-077: Since $X$ and $Y$ are bounded, $\bX$ and $\bY$ are compact.
elements.sent-2411-078: Then the desired inequalities for $X$ and $Y$ follow from Theorem~\ref{theorem:ineqMemoli and equalities $\dist_{GH (X,\,Y)=\dist_{GH (\bX,\,\bY)$, $\dist_{EH (X,\,Y)=\dist_{EH (\bX,\,\bY)$, $\diam\bX=\diam X$, $\diam\bY=\diam Y$.}}}}}
